title: Topic 135: The Psychology of Pre-Orders: Paying for Promises
content:
## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the curious case of the pre-order, a modern commercial ritual where consumers enthusiastically pay for the mere promise of a future product, often with the fervor of a squirrel burying nuts for a winter that may never come. We explore the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, from the sweet, sweet nectar of anticipatory utility to the cold, hard slap of the foot-in-the-door technique. Through a series of witty observations, real-world case studies of both pre-order triumphs and spectacular failures in the video game industry, and a smattering of actual academic research, we reveal why we are so eager to trade our hard-earned cash for a digital certificate of ‘dibs.’ The paper concludes that pre-ordering is less a rational economic decision and more a testament to the power of hope, hype, and the deep-seated human fear of missing out on the next big thing, even if that ‘thing’ turns out to be a buggy, unfinished mess.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand casino of consumerism, the pre-order is perhaps the most audacious bet. It is a wager placed not on a tangible item, but on a shimmering mirage of future gratification, a digital ghost in the machine. The modern consumer, a creature of supposed rationality, willingly hands over their currency for a product that, for all intents and purposes, does not yet exist. It is a leap of faith, a declaration of brand loyalty so profound it borders on the religious. We are, in essence, paying for promises, and business is booming.

This paper will dissect the pre-order phenomenon, particularly within the fertile and often fraught landscape of the video game industry. Here, pre-orders are not merely a sales tactic; they are a cultural event, a spectacle of hype and anticipation that can make or break a blockbuster title before a single copy is shipped. We will journey into the minds of the pre-ordering public, a demographic that ranges from the cautiously optimistic to the rabidly fanatical, to understand what compels them to open their wallets for a product that may be months, or even years, from release. Is it the allure of exclusive in-game trinkets? The fear of missing out on a cultural moment? Or is it something deeper, a psychological itch that only the promise of a new digital world can scratch?

We will examine the tools of the trade, the psychological levers that marketers so skillfully pull to separate us from our money. From the carefully orchestrated hype cycles that build to a fever pitch, to the subtle nudges of scarcity and social proof, we will reveal the invisible architecture of the pre-order machine. We will also explore the dark side of this phenomenon, the cautionary tales of pre-order disasters that have left a trail of broken promises and disgruntled gamers in their wake. Through a blend of academic rigor and a healthy dose of cynical humor, this paper aims to provide a comprehensive and entertaining exploration of the psychology of pre-orders, a modern marvel of marketing that has us all paying for promises.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not entirely ignored the peculiar dance of the pre-order. While the specific term may not echo through the hallowed halls of academia with the same frequency as, say, ‘cognitive dissonance’ or ‘Pavlovian response,’ the underlying psychological principles are well-documented. At the heart of the pre-order phenomenon lies the concept of **positive anticipatory utility**, a term that sounds like it was concocted in a sterile laboratory but simply means ‘the joy of waiting.’ Mukherjee, Smith, and Burton (2023) found that the pleasure derived from anticipating a future reward can be a powerful motivator, even more so than a simple price discount, especially when the wait is long. This explains why a gamer might be more enticed by the promise of an exclusive in-game skin than a few dollars off the price tag. The waiting itself becomes part of the product, a delicious foreplay to the main event.

Then there is the **foot-in-the-door phenomenon**, a classic of compliance psychology. First demonstrated by Freedman and Fraser in 1966, this technique involves getting a person to agree to a large request by having them agree to a modest request first. In the world of pre-orders, the modest request is the initial deposit, a seemingly innocuous gesture of commitment. But once that foot is in the door, the consumer is psychologically primed to follow through with the full purchase. As one blogger so eloquently put it, it’s ‘yessing your way through a no’ (Locke's Journey, 2015). The pre-order is not just a transaction; it’s a psychological contract, a commitment that we are loath to break.

Finally, we have the dynamic duo of **scarcity** and **FOMO (Fear Of Missing Out)**. The principle of scarcity dictates that we value things more when they are limited in availability. Marketers have weaponized this principle with ruthless efficiency, creating a sense of urgency with ‘limited time offers’ and ‘exclusive pre-order bonuses.’ This taps directly into our primal fear of missing out, a feeling so potent that it has its own acronym. As Mailchimp (n.d.) notes, FOMO marketing is about creating a sense of urgency and exclusivity, making consumers feel that if they don’t act now, they will be left out of the loop, a social pariah in a world of digital haves.

## ANALYSIS

So, how do these psychological principles manifest in the wild, specifically in the digital jungles of the video game industry? The pre-order campaign is a masterclass in applied psychology, a carefully orchestrated symphony of hype, desire, and fear. It begins with the **hype cycle**, a relentless barrage of trailers, developer diaries, and carefully curated gameplay demos. This is where the seeds of anticipatory utility are sown. The consumer is not just buying a game; they are buying into a narrative, a community, a shared experience. The longer the hype cycle, the more potent the anticipatory utility becomes. By the time the pre-order button appears, the consumer is not just clicking a button; they are consummating a long and passionate courtship.

The pre-order itself is a masterpiece of foot-in-the-door salesmanship. The initial deposit is a small price to pay for a piece of the future. But it is a powerful psychological anchor. The consumer has now made a commitment, and the pain of breaking that commitment, of admitting that they made a mistake, is often greater than the pain of parting with the rest of the money. This is reinforced by the constant stream of **social proof**. When a consumer sees that millions of others have pre-ordered a game, it validates their decision. They are not alone in their leap of faith; they are part of a movement, a tribe. The pre-order becomes a badge of honor, a symbol of their dedication to the cause.

And then there are the **pre-order bonuses**, the digital carrots dangled in front of the consumer. These are often trivial items – a special weapon, an exclusive outfit, a unique character skin – but they are imbued with the power of scarcity. They are available only to those who pre-order, a secret handshake for the initiated. This creates a two-tiered system of consumers: the pre-order elite and the common rabble who must wait for the standard edition. The fear of being in the latter category is a powerful motivator, a psychological cattle prod that drives consumers towards the pre-order button.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The video game industry is littered with the corpses of failed pre-order campaigns, cautionary tales of hype trains that derailed in spectacular fashion. The most infamous of these is, of course, **Cyberpunk 2077**. The game was the subject of an eight-year hype cycle, a marketing blitz of unprecedented scale and intensity. It generated 8 million pre-orders, a staggering number that translated to nearly half a billion dollars in revenue before the game was even released. But when the game finally launched, it was a technical disaster, a buggy, unfinished mess that was virtually unplayable on last-gen consoles. The backlash was swift and brutal. Refunds were offered, apologies were made, and the game was even temporarily removed from the PlayStation Store. The developers, CD Projekt Red, were accused of deliberately concealing the poor state of the console versions, of selling a promise that they knew they couldn’t keep (Collider, 2021).

But for every Cyberpunk 2077, there is a pre-order success story, a game that delivered on its promises and rewarded the faith of its early adopters. The **Final Fantasy VII Remake**, for example, was another highly anticipated title with a long development cycle. But when it was released, it was a critical and commercial success, a polished and well-crafted game that exceeded the expectations of many fans. The pre-order campaign for this game was a success not because of any particularly clever marketing tricks, but because the developers had a track record of delivering high-quality games, and they ultimately delivered on their promises.

These examples highlight the double-edged sword of the pre-order. When a developer can be trusted to deliver a quality product, the pre-order can be a win-win for both the consumer and the developer. The consumer gets to be a part of the hype and excitement, and the developer gets a valuable early revenue stream. But when that trust is broken, the pre-order can become a symbol of corporate greed and deception, a cautionary tale that echoes through the gaming community for years to come.

## DISCUSSION

The psychology of pre-orders has profound implications for our understanding of consumer behavior. It demonstrates that we are not the rational, utility-maximizing creatures that classical economics would have us believe. We are emotional, impulsive, and deeply susceptible to the power of suggestion. We are more swayed by the promise of a future reward than by the cold, hard logic of a discount. We are more afraid of missing out than we are of losing our money. And we are more likely to follow through with a purchase if we have already made a small commitment, no matter how irrational that purchase may be.

For the gaming industry, the pre-order is a powerful tool, but it is also a dangerous one. It has allowed developers to generate massive amounts of revenue before a game is even finished, which can be a valuable source of funding. But it has also created a perverse incentive to prioritize marketing over development, to sell the sizzle rather than the steak. The Cyberpunk 2077 debacle is a stark reminder of the dangers of this approach. When a company takes its customers’ trust for granted, the consequences can be devastating, not just for the company’s bottom line, but for the industry as a whole.

Ultimately, the pre-order is a reflection of the unique relationship between gamers and the games they play. Games are not just products; they are experiences, worlds, and communities. The pre-order is a way for gamers to invest in those experiences, to become a part of the story before it even begins. It is a testament to the power of games to inspire passion, loyalty, and a willingness to take a leap of faith. But it is also a reminder that with great power comes great responsibility. Developers who abuse that trust do so at their own peril.

## CONCLUSION

In conclusion, the pre-order is a fascinating and complex phenomenon, a testament to the enduring power of hope and hype in the face of uncertainty. It is a dance between the rational and the irrational, the calculated and the impulsive. It is a world where the promise of a future reward can be more valuable than the reward itself, and where the fear of missing out can be a more powerful motivator than the fear of being ripped off. The pre-order is a mirror that reflects our own psychological quirks and biases, our hopes and our fears, our capacity for both blind faith and righteous indignation.

As consumers, we would be wise to approach the pre-order with a healthy dose of skepticism, to remember that we are paying for a promise, not a product. And as for the developers, they would do well to remember that trust, once broken, is not easily repaired. The pre-order may be a powerful tool, but it is a tool that should be wielded with care, lest it become a weapon that turns against its wielder. After all, in the grand casino of consumerism, the house doesn’t always win.
references:
Mukherjee, A., Smith, R. J., & Burton, S. (2023). The effect of positive anticipatory utility on product pre-order evaluations and choices. *Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science*, *51*(1), 551–569. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11747-021-00810-1

Locke's Journey. (2015, October 13). The Psychology of Hype and Pre-Order Culture. Retrieved from https://lockesjourney.wordpress.com/2015/10/13/the-psychology-of-hype-and-pre-order-culture/

Reimann, T. (2021, January 7). Cyberpunk 2077 Pre-Order: Was It Worth It or Not? Collider. Retrieved from https://collider.com/cyberpunk-2077-pre-order-worth-it/

Mailchimp. (n.d.). FOMO in Marketing: Scarcity Sells. Retrieved from https://mailchimp.com/resources/fomo-marketing/
